chunk_summary: Guidelines for first-line micronutrient tests and escalation triggers in clinical settings, focusing on anemia, macrocytosis, rickets, hypothyroidism, and zinc deficiency management.
learning_objectives: Understand the appropriate first-line tests for anemia, macrocytosis, rickets, hypothyroidism, and zinc deficiency.
Learn escalation triggers for urgent micronutrient-related symptoms such as neurologic regression, hypocalcemic seizures, and severe anemia.
Recognize the limitations of broad micronutrient panels and unreliable markers like serum zinc and urinary iodine.
key_facts: For anemia: Start with CBC, reticulocyte count, and pair ferritin with CRP/AGP; avoid serum iron alone.
For macrocytosis: Order serum folate and B12; escalate urgently for neurologic symptoms and avoid folate alone without B12.
For rickets: Use a bone profile (calcium, phosphate, alkaline phosphatase, albumin, creatinine, magnesium) and radiographs for deformities; reserve 25(OH)D and PTH for specific cases.
For hypothyroidism: Order TSH and free T4; consider thyroid antibodies for suspected autoimmune thyroiditis.
Serum zinc is unreliable due to diurnal variation and inflammation; manage with diet correction and time-limited trials.
reference_quote: If testing, pair ferritin with an inflammation marker (CRP and/or AGP) and avoid using serum iron alone. — WHO guidelines on ferritin concentrations for iron status assessment.
document_key_topics: First-line micronutrient tests for clinical management
Escalation triggers for urgent micronutrient deficiencies
Limitations of broad micronutrient panels and unreliable markers
